text: The surgeon inserts a trocar directly into the umbilicus, preparing for laparoscopic access.
The surgeon uses an aspiration needle to confirm the presence of cystic fluid, then proceeds to aspirate and remove the fluid from the cyst.
The surgeon uses a 5mm trocar and suction device to aspirate the maximum amount of fluid from the cyst.
The abdominal cavity is visibly filled with cystic fluid following the rupture, with fluid collected in various quadrants of the abdomen.
The surgeon uses a suction device to aspirate the remaining spilled cystic fluid from the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon uses a fiber suction device with high suction pressure to aspirate the remaining cystic fluid from the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon uses a 45-liter insufflator to maintain pneumoperitoneum while suctioning cystic fluid from the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon identifies multiple pockets of cystic fluid remaining in the abdominal cavity after initial suctioning, indicating areas that still require fluid removal.
The surgeon observes a multiloculated cyst, characterized by multiple distinct fluid-filled pockets within the cyst structure.
The multiloculated cyst is visually separated by septa, revealing distinct compartments within the cystic structure.
The surgeon holds and punctures additional cysts within the surgical field to create space for further surgical maneuvers.
The surgeon aspirates fluid from another compartment of the multiloculated cyst, maintaining the pneumoperitoneum using a 45-liter insufflator.
The surgeon uses suction to aspirate fluid from the fourth cyst, which has been punctured, and proceeds to address the fifth cyst, revealing multiple cystic pockets.
The surgeon uses a trocar inserted into the umbilicus to aspirate fluid from a large cyst, creating space for instrument movement. A vibrating jaw of the harmonic device is visible near another cyst.

The surgeon uses a vibrating jaw of the harmonic device to drill near the fallopian tube, minimizing the risk of remote injury due to the absence of electrical current. The fallopian tube is visible, and the surgeon holds it to gain a clear view of the infundibulopelvic ligament.
The surgeon holds the cystic valve to determine its orientation while using suction irrigation to remove a large volume of fluid from the adjacent septal compartments.
The sigmoidal colon is visualized at the level of the infundibulopelvic ligament during the procedure.
The surgeon opens the outer ovarian cortex, revealing a thick wall due to previous stretching. A septum within the ovarian tissue is identified.
The surgeon applies intramedial traction to expose the infundibulopelvic ligament, which is visible in the surgical field.
The sigmoidal colon is visible on the left side below the infundibulopelvic ligament.
The surgeon opens the outer ovarian cortex, revealing the infundibulopelvic ligament clearly within the surgical field.
The surgeon visually identifies the infundibulopelvic ligament on the left side, highlighting its anatomical location within the surgical field.
A small amount of omentum is visible being added to the surgical field.
The surgeon positions the instruments close to the cyst, maintaining proximity to facilitate precise dissection and minimize risk to surrounding structures.
The surgeon separates the cystic wall and pulls it towards the upper abdomen. A colpotomy is performed at the posterior fornix, with visible suctioning to clear the surgical field.
The left ovary and fallopian tube are visible, along with the right ovary. The surgeon carefully pushes the tissue into the posterior fornix to facilitate the colpotomy procedure.

A sponge is placed inside the abdomen, followed by the use of claw forceps to grasp the cystic wall. The surgeon performs manual morselation to facilitate the removal of the cyst through the vagina, ensuring careful handling during the process.
The claw forceps are used to pull the cyst through the posterior colpotomy, visibly manipulating the cystic wall during the extraction process.
The surgeon punctures the large, multiloculated septa within the cystic structure, using an appropriate instrument to facilitate drainage or further manipulation.
The surgeon aspirates the cystic contents using a syringe or suction device, visibly reducing the cyst's size.
The surgeon displays the mucinous content extracted from the cyst, showcasing its gelatinous appearance.
The surgeon observes the collapsed cyst, indicating that its removal will be easier now that it has been deflated.
The surgeon carefully removes the collapsed cyst, gradually separating it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon ruptures the cyst, releasing its mucinous content, which is visible as it spills into the surgical field.
The surgeon slowly pulls on the cyst, causing it to rupture, revealing another cyst beneath.
The surgeon punctures the cyst, allowing its contents to drain, and then carefully removes the cyst from the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon performs careful suction to remove fluid and debris from the surgical field, ensuring clear visibility of the cystectomy procedure.
The surgeon carefully extracts the cyst, which is slowly being removed from the surgical field.
The surgeon begins to pull up the cyst, applying gentle traction to elevate it from the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon assists in rupturing another cyst, applying pressure to facilitate its release.
The cyst is gradually extracted from the surgical site, with visible careful manipulation to ensure complete removal.
The cyst is fully extracted from the surgical site.
The video shows a large cyst with multiple septa visible within its structure.
The surgeon performs a thorough lavage of the surgical area following the removal of the cyst, ensuring the cavity is cleaned and free of debris.
